coredata.dc:title: Lycium barbarum oligosaccharide-derived carbon quantum dots inhibit glial scar formation while promoting neuronal differentiation of neural stem cells

coredata.dc:description: Overexpression of glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) in activated astrocytes following spinal cord injury is closely associated with glial scar formation, which harms axonal regrowth. In this study, we prepared ultrasmall cationic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Lycium barbarum oligosaccharides were used as the carbon source for the first time, and polyetherimide (PEI) and ethylenediamine (ED) were used as cationic reagents. Interestingly, the resultant CQDs show the bioactivity of specifically inhibiting GFAP protein expression, while promoting neuronal marker expression in neural stem cells (NSCs). Furthermore, CQDs together with NSCs can remarkably improve the motor activity of animals after implantation into the transection lesion of the rat spinal cord. Histological analysis confirmed that CQDs can enhance neuronal differentiation of NSCs while inhibiting glial scar formation in vivo. Altogether, this study represents the first report of producing CQDs from oligosaccharides and investigating their impact on NSCs differentiation, thus providing a paradigm for exploring the bioactivity of quantum dots.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Culture of neural stem cells and astrocytes
NSCs differentiation induced by CQDs in vitro
Evaluation of the effect of CQDs on GFAP expression in astrocytes in vitro
L. barbarum oligosaccharide (LBOS) is isolated and characterized
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared from LBOS via one-step hydrothermal carbonization
CQDs are characterized by a series of analysis
Differentiation of NSCs is induced by CQDs in vitro
GFAP expression in astrocytes was specifically inhibited by CQDs in vitro
CQDs promote axonal regeneration and function recovery after SCI in rats

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Lycium barbarum saccharide. (a) The high-performance gel permeation chromatography (HPGPC) spectrum of the refined Lycium barbarum saccharide; (b) the mass spectrum of the Lycium barbarum saccharide; (c) chromatograms of the monosaccharide composition of the standard substance mixture and LBOS, respectively.

Characterization of CQDs. (a) Photograph of the luminescent CQDs solution under a 365-nm UV lamp excitation, using double distilled water as control; (b) UV–vis absorption (line a), excitation (line b), and photoluminescent emission (line c, excited at 390 nm) spectra of the CQDs; (c) excitation-dependent photoluminescent emission spectra of the CQDs recorded with different excitation wavelengths increasing from 360 to 440 nm, with 10 nm increments; (d) FT-IR spectra of LBOS and CQDs; (e) high-resolution TEM images and diameter distribution of the CQDs (insert); (f) Agarose gel electrophoresis patterns of CQDs/pDNA complexes at different weight ratios, from left to right, well 1: plasmid DNA marker, well 2: free plasmid EGFP, wells 3–16: CQDs/pEGFP complexes with weight ratios of 0.2:1, 0.4:1, 0.6:1, 0.8:1, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 30:1, 40:1, and 50:1, respectively.

NSCs migration and differentiation induced by CQDs. (a) A bright field image of neurospheres (a1), and the spheres that were immunofluorescence stained against Nestin (a2) and Sox2 (a3). The nuclei were counterstained with Hoechst 33258 (a4). Scale bars, 200 μm. (b) Cytotoxicity of CQDs on NSCs. (c) CQDs entered the NSCs, and emitted bright blue, weak green, and almost no red fluorescence under wavelengths at 405, 488, and 514 nm, respectively. Scale bars, 50 μm. (d) The morphology of neurospheres and the out-migrating cells after 4 days of treatment with LBOS and CQDs. Scale bars, 100 μm.

CQDs inhibit GFAP expression while promoting neuronal differentiation of NSCs. (a) Double staining with GFAP/Tuj1 and GFAP/MAP2 to identify the astroglial and neuronal differentiation of NSCs after treatment with CQDs. All cells were labeled blue with CQDs when excited at 405 nm. Scale bars, 100 μm. (b) Percentage of neurons (Tuj1+/GFAP-, MAP2+/GFAP-) and astrocytes (GFAP+) after 4 days of treatment by CQDs with various concentrations (0, 10, 20, and 50 μg/mL). (c) Western blot and its corresponding bar graphs showing the expression of neuronal markers Tuj1 and MAP2 and astroglial marker GFAP in NSCs with/without LBOS or CQDs treatment. **, P < 0.01, **** P < 0.0001, versus control.

RNA transcriptome sequencing. (a) Sample cluster analysis. There were three parallel samples in the NSC control group and CQDS-treated NSCs (NSC_CQS) group. (b)The number of genes up- and down-regulated after treatment with CQDs. (c) Heatmap showing the differential genes of NSCs with and without CQD treatment.

CQDs show specificity in suppressing GFAP expression. (a–f) GFAP and MAP2 immunofluorescence staining after treatment. (a) Control; (b) CQDs prepared with PEI (PEI-CQDs); (c) CQDs prepared with ED (ED-CQDs); (d) LBOS; (e) CQDs prepared with LBOS and PEI; (f) CQDs prepared with LBOS and ED. All cells were labeled blue with CQDs when excited at 405 nm. Scale bars, 100 μm. (g) Immunofluorescence staining of β-tubulin/GFAP, vimentin/GFAP, and phalloidin/GFAP in rat astrocytes after treatment with CQDs at various concentrations (0, 20, 50, and 100 μg/mL). All cells were labeled blue with CQDs when excited at 405 nm. Scale bars, 10 μm. Quantitative analysis showed that the percentage of GFAP+ astrocytes decreased significantly after the application of CQDs. Data are presented as mean ± SEM, determined by unpaired t-test and plotted using GraphPad software. *, P < 0.05, **, P < 0.01, compared with control (0 mg/mL CQDs); #, P < 0.05, ##, P < 0.01, compared with 20 mg/mL CQDs.

CQDs promote functional recovery after spinal cord injury in rats. (a) Movement trajectories of rats that had received different treatments after SCI, including non-treatment control (SCI group), fibrin matrix only (SCI+F group), fibrin matrix and CQDs (SCI+F group), fibrin matrix and NSCs (SCI+F+N group), fibrin matrix, NSCs and CQDs (SCI+F+N+C group), and fibrin matrix and CQDs-pretreated NSCs (SCI+F+CN group). (b-e) Parameters including time moving (b), total distance (c), center distance (d), and time CW/time CCW (e) reveal the locomotor activity of rats. *P < 0.05; **P < 0.01 versus SCI group.

CQDs facilitate axonal re-growth after spinal cord injury in rats. (a) Immunostained spinal sections of the SCI model with GFAP (green) and NF-H (red). Scar bar, 2 mm. (b-f) Immunostained spinal sections implanted with fibrin matrix only (SCI+F group) (b), fibrin matrix and CQDs (SCI+C group) (c), fibrin matrix and NSCs (SCI+F+N group) (d), fibrin matrix, NSCs, and CQDs (SCI+F+N+C group) (e), and fibrin matrix and CQDs-pretreated NSCs (SCI+F+CN group) (f), respectively. The specimens were stained against GFAP (green) and NF-H (red). Scar bars, 500 μm. The dotted lines indicate the lesion border. Stars indicate the expression of GFAP. Arrows show the extension of neurofilaments across the lesion gap.

head.ce:title: Lycium barbarum oligosaccharide-derived carbon quantum dots inhibit glial scar formation while promoting neuronal differentiation of neural stem cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Overexpression of glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) in activated astrocytes following spinal cord injury is closely associated with glial scar formation, which harms axonal regrowth. In this study, we prepared ultrasmall cationic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Lycium barbarum oligosaccharides were used as the carbon source for the first time, and polyetherimide (PEI) and ethylenediamine (ED) were used as cationic reagents. Interestingly, the resultant CQDs show the bioactivity of specifically inhibiting GFAP protein expression, while promoting neuronal marker expression in neural stem cells (NSCs). Furthermore, CQDs together with NSCs can remarkably improve the motor activity of animals after implantation into the transection lesion of the rat spinal cord. Histological analysis confirmed that CQDs can enhance neuronal differentiation of NSCs while inhibiting glial scar formation in vivo. Altogether, this study represents the first report of producing CQDs from oligosaccharides and investigating their impact on NSCs differentiation, thus providing a paradigm for exploring the bioactivity of quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a devastating condition with instantaneous impairments of motor, sensory, and autonomic functions below the level of trauma [1]. Despite the advances in rehabilitative, cellular, and molecular therapies, limited therapeutic effects have been achieved due to the complexity of the pathological changes following SCI, such as hemorrhage, oxidative stress, edema, massive death of neurons and glia, loss of axons, infiltration of immune cells, and lack of neurotrophic factors [2,3]. Neural stem cell (NSC) therapy represents an attractive alternative for spinal cord repair, owning to its potential of replacing damaged neuronal and glial cells, producing neurotrophic factors, bridging lesion cavities, remyelinating spared axons and restoring neuronal circuity [4,5]. However, the therapeutic effects of NSCs are compromised in clinical treatments because of the low survival rate of the transplanted NSCs and their differentiation tendency into hypertrophic astrocytes in the lesion site. This situation forms a physical barrier composed of an astroglial scar that inhibits neuronal circuity restoration [6]. Hence, inhibiting the glial differentiation of NSCs might improve the therapeutic efficiency of NSC therapy for SCI.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a physical dimension of 1–10 nm, were first discovered serendipitously during the preparation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) in 2004 [7] and received their scientific designation as CQDs in 2006 [8]. Currently, there are two primary strategies for the synthesis of CQDs, namely, “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [9]. The “top-down” approach often involves harsh splitting methods of breaking down a bulk carbon source such as orange peel [10], banana peel [11], and carbon fibers [12] through electronic carbonization, chemical ablation, and laser ablation. By contrast, the “bottom-up” approach, which is based on milder methods such as hydrothermal treatment and microwave irradiation, allows the basic building block (i.e., small organic molecules) to be carbonized, condensed, and polymerized into fluorescent CQDs [9]. The “top-down” route is short of precise control of the size and morphology of the resulting CQDs, while the “bottom-up” method allows for more accurate regulation of the properties of the final products [13]. So far, a bunch of low-molecular materials such as citric acid, glycerol, glucose, and amino acids have been used as carbon sources for preparing CQDs with the “bottom-up” route [14,15]. However, utilizing oligosaccharides as the carbon source for CQDs production has not been reported.

CQDs have recently gained increasing attention due to their numerous advantages over organic dyes and semiconductive quantum dots, such as good biocompatibility, low toxicity, a broad range of excitation spectra, tunable emission spectra, excellent photostability against blinking and photobleaching, and environmental compatibility [16,17]. In addition to the extensive investigation on the application of CQDs in multiple fields, including bioimaging, biosensing, therapy, energy conversion, drug/gene delivery, and anti-microbic [18–21], increasing attention has been paid to their biological functions such as promoting wound healing [22], anti-inflammation [23], and antioxidation [24].

Herein, we report a “bottom-up” route for preparing blue-photoluminescent CQDs with ultra-small size (∼1 nm) and positive charge through an eco-friendly, one-step hydrothermal carbonization reaction, using an oligosaccharide from Lycium barbarum as the carbon source and a mixture of polyetherimide (PEI) and ethylenediamine (ED) as the cationic reagents. Lycium barbarum has long been used as an anti-aging herb in traditional Chinese medicine [25]. As one of the foremost effective ingredients, Lycium barbarum polysaccharides have shown positive effects on nerve repair and regeneration following cavernous nerve injury, as previously reported [26,27]. Moreover, Lycium barbarum oligosaccharides (LBOS) were reported to enhance the therapeutic effect of nasal mucosa-derived mesenchymal stem cells (EMSCs) on spinal cord injury via M2 (anti-inflammatory phenotype) polarization of microglia [28]. Nevertheless, whether the LBOS-derived CQDs can contribute to nerve regeneration remains unknown. Herein, for the first time, CQDs were prepared with LBOS, and their biological function in terms of regulating NSCs differentiation was examined both in vitro and in rat injured spinal cord. Altogether, this work might expand our understanding of the function and application of CQDs, and offer a potential inhibitor of glial scar formation to promote recovery of neural function after SCI.

DMEM/Ham's F-12 (1:1) (DF-12) medium, neurobasal medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), B27, GlutaMAX, non-essential amino acids (NEAA), penicillin, streptomycin, and trypsin were purchased from Gibco BRL (Invitrogen Co., Carlsbad, CA, USA). Basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF), epidermal growth factor (EGF), brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), and neurotrophin-3 (NT-3) were provided by PeproTech (PeproTech, USA). Branched poly(ethylenimine) solution (PEI; molecular weight 1.3 KDa), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), bisbenzimide H 33258, heparin, poly-L-ornithin, laminin and fibrinogen were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Poly-l-lysine was purchased from Nanjing Aoduofuni Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Thrombin was obtained from Shanghai Gaochuang Medical Technology Co., Ltd. Primary antibodies including anti-glial fibrillary acidic protein (anti-GFAP) and anti-Nestin were obtained from Millipore (Boston, MA, USA); anti-β-actin, anti-Tuj1, anti-Sox2, anti-vimentin, and anti-tubulin were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, UK); anti-MAP2 and anti-NF-H were provided by ProteinTech (Wuhan, Hubei, China). Secondary antibodies including goat-anti-mouse IgG-HRP, goat-anti-rabbit IgG-HRP, goat-anti-mouse IgG-Alexa Fluor® 488 were purchased from Fcmacs (Nanjing, Jiangsu, China); and goat anti-mouse IgG-Cy3 was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Lycium barbarum L. was obtained from Zhenjiang Zhilin Pharmacy (Zhenjiang, China). Ethylenediamine, petroleum ether, ethanol, chloroform, n-butyl alcohol, ammonium acetate, quinine, and sulfuric acid solution were all purchased from Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. of China National Pharmaceutical Group (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. AB-8 and D315 macroporous adsorption resins were obtained from Shanghai Luyuan Biological Co., Ltd. DEAE-52 cellulose was obtained from Beijing Ruida Henghui Technology Development Co., Ltd. Deionized water was prepared by Milli-Q water system (Milford, MA, USA).

The Lycium barbarum L. was dried, pulverized, and refluxed with petroleum ether to remove the fat-soluble substances. Then the Lycium barbarum L saccharides were extracted with deionized water at 80 °C (solvent-to-solid ratio of 1:10, w/v) for 3 h each time, followed by filtration to remove impurities. The filtrates were combined and precipitated with 80 % ethanol overnight. The resultant precipitate was then collected by centrifugation, washed thrice with absolute ethanol, and redissolved in distilled water. Afterward, the Savage method (chloroform: n-butyl alcohol = 5:1, repeat 5–8 times) was employed to remove proteins [29], and the aqueous phase was collected.

Next, the crude saccharide was separated sequentially by column chromatography with AB-8 macroporous adsorption resins [30], D315 macroporous adsorption resins [31], and DEAE-52 cellulose [32]. Briefly, the lyophilized crude saccharides of Lycium barbarum L. were dissolved in deionized water (0.5 g/mL) before use. Then, 1 mL of the saccharide solution was discolored and separated using an AB-8 macroporous adsorption resin column (Φ4.0 cm × 50 cm) with deionized water. The eluate was concentrated by rotary evaporation to a volume of 1 mL and then applied to a D315 macroporous adsorption resin column (Φ4.0 cm × 50 cm) and eluted with deionized water. The eluent was collected and monitored peak shape and retention time by high-performance gel permeation chromatography (HPGPC) which was carried out with a TSKgel G4000PWXL (7.8 mm×300 mm, 10 μm) column on an Agilent 1260 system with evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD). The HPGPC condition was as follows: the flow rate of the mobile phase was 0.6 mL/min with 20 mM ammonium acetate, while the flow rate of the carrier gas was 1.6 mL/min with high-purity nitrogen. The temperature of the column incubator and detector was kept at 30 °C.

Elutes with the same retention time were combined and concentrated by rotary evaporation to a volume of 0.5 mL. Then, the relatively pure saccharides were performed on a DEAE-52 cellulose column (Φ3.0 cm × 45 cm) with deionized water to increase the purity. Finally, a single symmetric peak was obtained, and the eluates were collected and freeze-dried to obtain the refined LBOS.

The LBOS was dissolved in deionized water and prepared into a 10 mg/mL solution before use. The Phenol‑sulfuric acid assay [33] and sulfuric acid-carbazole analysis were performed to determine the content of saccharides and uronic acids [34], respectively. The residual protein was determined by the Bradford assay (Solarbio, Beijing, China), and the molecular weight of LBOS was measured by ion trap mass spectrometry (Thermo LXQ). According to the previous report, the monosaccharide composition of LBOS was analyzed using the previously reported 1-phenyl-3-methyl-5-pyrazolone (PMP) pre-column derivatization HPLC method [35]. The glycosidic linkage position of LBOS was evaluated via periodate oxidation [36]. The triple-helix structure of LBOS was determined with the Congo red method [29].

The CQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal carbonization route with the refined LBOS as the carbon source and PEI and ethylenediamine as cationic reagents. The reaction conditions were as follows: LBOS (15 mg in 50 μL deionized water), ethylenediamine (70 μL), and PEI (30 μL) were mixed in a 0.5-mL centrifuge tube, which was then sealed and heated at 100 °C for 3 h. The mixture was vortexed every 30 min during the heating period. After the reactant naturally cooled to ambient temperature, 4 mL of deionized water was added to the sample, followed by rotary evaporation at 60 °C to remove the volatile ethylenediamine. The resulting solution was dialyzed (MW cut-off 300 Da, Union Carbide Corporation, USA) against deionized water in a clean container for 24 h. The external dialysate was collected, concentrated, filtered (0.22-μm filter), and freeze-dried (Christ Alpha 2–4, Germany) to obtain the fluorescent CQDs.

The fluorescence of the resultant CQDs was observed with a ZF-2 Ultraviolet Analyzer (Shanghai Anting Electronic Instrument Factory, China). The absorbance spectra of the CQDs were determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, SHIMADZU, Japan). The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were recorded using a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, SHIMADZU, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were observed using an AVATAR 370 (Nicolet, USA) in the frequency range of 500–4000 cm−1. The 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AvanceII spectrometer (400 MHz) at 30 °C for 30 min. The total amount of nitrogen in LBOS was detected with a Euro EA elemental analyzer (EURO VECTOR Instruments and Software, Milan, Italy).

The microstructure and particle size of the CQDs were measured on a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Philips-FEI, Holland) with an accelerating voltage of 50–300 kV.

Agarose gel electrophoresis was used to detect the charge property of the CQDs. Briefly, the CQDs were prepared into a solution of 1 mg/mL with deionized water before use. As a model gene, plasmid EGFP (pEGFP) was diluted to obtain a solution of 0.1 mg/mL. Then the CQDs and pEGFP were prepared into complexes with their respective weight ratios (0.2:1, 0.4:1, 0.6:1, 0.8:1, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 30:1, 40:1 and 50:1) by adding an equal volume of pEGFP solution to the CQDs solution. The mixtures (10 μL each) were kept at room temperature (25 °C) for 30 min and then mixed with 2 μL of loading buffer (0.005 % bromophenol blue, 0.1 % sodium dodecyl sulfate and 5 % glycerol) and separated with 1 % agarose gel that contained 0.1 % ethidium bromide (EtBr) in a Trisborate-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid buffer solution (TBE, pH 8.0) at 80 V for 1 h. The gel was imaged with a UV transilluminator (Gel Doc 2000, BIO-RAD Laboratories, Hercules, CA) at a wavelength of 365 nm.

The fluorescence quantum yields of the CQDs were detected by comparing their integrated photoluminescence intensity as previously reported [37]. Quinine sulfate is a commonly used standard material for the fluorescence quantum yield. Its quantum yield in 0.1 M H2SO4 is 54 % (the excitation light is 350 nm, and the emission light ranges from 400 nm to 600 nm). Using this solution as a reference, the CQDs were dissolved in deionized water at a variety of concentrations. The photoluminescence emission spectra of samples were measured at an excitation wavelength corresponding to the UV range using an RF-5310 spectrofluorophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). The integrated photoluminescence intensity is the area under the photoluminescence curve over the excitation wavelength range. The quantum yields were calculated according to the following equation:
Yu =
Ys ∗
Fu / Fs
∗
As / Au
where Yu is the quantum yield of the CQDs and Ys is the quantum yield of quinine sulfate; Fu is the integrated fluorescence intensities of the CQDs, and Fs is the integrated fluorescence intensities of quinine sulfate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture of neural stem cells and astrocytes
ce:section.ce:para: The NSCs were isolated from the brains of neonatal SD rats (1 day postnatal, no gender limitation, provided by the laboratory animal research center of Jiangsu University) using a previous method with some modifications [2]. All animal experimental protocols were approved by the Jiangsu University Ethics Committee on animal experiments, in conformity with the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals (UJS-IACUC-2019100902) and the National Institutes of Health guide for the care and use of Laboratory animals (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978). In brief, the whole brains were surgically dissected and placed in Petri dishes containing phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) supplemented with penicillin-streptomycin (100 U/mL), followed by removing cerebellums, meninges, and blood vessels under aseptic conditions. Then the rest of the brain tissue was cut into small pieces (1 mm3) and digested with 0.025 % trypsin at 37 °C for 3–5 min. The single-cell suspension was collected and centrifugated at 1500 rpm for 5 min. The cell precipitate was collected and washed twice with PBS solution and then cultured with NSCs culture medium (DF-12 medium, 48 mL; N2, 0.25 mL; B27, 1 mL; GlutaMAX, 0.5 mL; NEAA, 0.5 mL; bFGF, 20 ng/mL; EGF, 20 ng/mL; heparin, 2 μg/mL; penicillin, 100 U/mL; streptomycin, 100 U/mL). The medium was changed every 3 days. One week later, spherical neurospheres were formed and identified by immunofluorescence assay against Nestin and Sox2.

Astrocytes were prepared from the brains of the above-mentioned neonatal SD rats according to previous reports with minor modifications [2]. Briefly, the brain cortices were dissected, and the cerebellum, meninges, blood vessels, and hippocampus were removed. The remaining cortical tissue was trypsinized (0.025 %) at 37 °C for 10 min. The cell pellets were collected by centrifugation at 1500 rpm for 5 min, resuspended in DF-12 medium containing 10 % FBS (v/v), and cultured in dishes pretreated with 0.1 mg/mL poly-l-lysine. One week later, relatively pure astrocytes with monolayer growth were formed and identified by immunofluorescence assay against GFAP.

NSCs were digested into a single-cell suspension and plated on 96-well plates (pretreated overnight with 0.1 mg/mL cm-1polylysine (PLL)) at a density of 1×104 cells per well and cultured at 37 °C under a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere. When the cells reached 40 % confluence, the culture medium in each well was replaced with fresh DF-12/10 % FBS containing CQDs with a variety of concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, and 300 μg/mL), followed by incubation for 8 days at 37 °C. The medium was changed every 2 days. Four days later, an aliquot (20 μL) of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and further incubated for 4 h. The medium was removed, and 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to each well. The plate was gently vortexed for 5 min to completely dissolve the formazan crystal. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm with an Epoch Microplate Reader (BioTek, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NSCs differentiation induced by CQDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Neurospheres were seeded in a 24-well plate (pretreated overnight with 10 μg/mL laminin and 0.01 % poly-L-ornithine at 4 °C) at a density of 20–30 neurospheres per well and maintained in DF-12/1 % FBS medium at 37 °C under a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere. After the neurospheres adhered to the bottom of the well, the culture medium was replaced with neuronal induction medium (neurobasal medium, 24.5 mL; DF-12 medium, 24.5 mL; N2, 0.25 mL; B27, 0.5 mL; bFGF, 10 ng/mL; BDNF, 20 ng/mL; NT3, 20 ng/mL; GlutaMAX, 0.5 mL; penicillin, 100 U/mL; streptomycin, 100 U/mL) containing different concentrations of the CQDs (0, 10, 20 and 50 μg/mL). The medium was changed every 4 days. Meanwhile, the original LBOS and CQDs derived from both LBOS+PEI and LBOS+ ethylenediamine served as the control groups. After 4 days of induction, an immunofluorescence assay was performed to detect the expression of the marker of astrocytes (GFAP), immature neurons (Tuj1), and mature neurons (MAP2), respectively.

Cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 30 min and rinsed three times with PBS. Then, the cells were permeabilized and blocked with 0.1 % Triton/5 % BSA in PBS for 30 min. After washing thrice with PBS, cells were incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies (Table S2) diluted in primary antibody staining buffer. The following day, cells were washed three times with PBS and then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h with secondary antibodies diluted with primary antibody staining buffer according to the manufacturer's instructions. Afterward, the secondary antibodies were aspirated, and cells were incubated for 30 min with Hoechst33258 (1 μM) for the nucleus counterstain. After washing three times with PBS, images were taken using a Leica epifluorescence light microscope (Leica Microsystems, Wetzlar, Germany). As a control, the group without immunofluorescence staining was also observed under the same conditions to exclude the fluorescence of the CQDs themselves.

Cells were lysed using RIPA lysis buffer (Beyotime, Shanghai, China) containing PMSF and cocktail at 4 °C for 30 min. After centrifugation at 12000 g for 5 min, the supernatant was collected and mixed with an equal amount of loading buffer, and heated at 100 °C for 10 min to denature the proteins. Then, each sample was separated using 12 % sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and transferred to a polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane (Millipore, Temecula, CA, USA). The PVDF membrane was blocked using 5 % BSA in TBST (20 mM Tris/HCl, 137 mM NaCl, 0.4 % Tween 20, pH 7.6) for 1 h, followed by sequential incubation with primary antibodies (listed in Table S2) overnight at 4 °C. Then, the membrane was immersed in HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies (Fcmacs, Nanjing, China). Finally, the sample bands were visualized using a Pierce ECL Plus substrate (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and scanned with a Typhoon 9400 Variable Mode Imager (Amersham Biosciences, UK).

NSCs were seeded in a 24-well plate and incubated with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for four days as described in 2.9. Neurospheres without CQDs were used as controls. Total RNA was extracted using a Trizol reagent kit per the manufacturer's protocol. RNA quality was assessed on an Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA) and checked using RNase-free agarose gel electrophoresis. After total RNA was extracted, eukaryotic mRNA was enriched by Oligo (dT) beads. Then, the enriched mRNA was fragmented into short fragments using a fragmentation buffer and reversely transcribed into cDNA using an NEBNext Ultra RNA Library Prep Kit for Illumina (NEB #7530, New England Biolabs, Ipswich, MA, USA). The purified double-stranded cDNA fragments were end-repaired, and a base was added and ligated to Illumina sequencing adapters. The ligation reaction was purified with the AMPure XP Beads (1.0×). Ligated fragments were subjected to size selection by agarose gel electrophoresis, and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) was amplified. The resulting cDNA library was sequenced using Illumina Novaseq 6000, and the subsequent bioinformatic analyses were performed by Gene Denovo Biotechnology Co. (Guangzhou, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the effect of CQDs on GFAP expression in astrocytes in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Astrocytes were plated on 96-well plates (pretreated overnight with 0.1 mg/mL PLL) at a density of 1×104 cells per well and cultured at 37 °C under a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere. When the cells reached 40 % confluence, the culture medium in each well was replaced with fresh DF-12/10 % FBS containing CQDs with a variety of concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, and 300 μg/mL), followed by incubation for 8 days at 37 °C. The medium was changed every 4 days. Then, an aliquot (20 μL) of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and further incubated for 4 h. The medium was removed, and 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to each well. The plate was gently vortexed for 5 min to completely dissolve the formazan crystal. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm with an Epoch Microplate Reader (BioTek, USA).

A total of 18 adult SD rats (weighing 200–220 g, male) were provided by the laboratory animal center of Jiangsu University. These animals all underwent complete transection lesions of the T9 spinal cord, and 2-mm spinal cord tissue was removed as previously described [2]. Immediately after lesioning, the rats were randomly divided into 6 major groups (3 rats per group): group 1, rats underwent spinal cord transection injury without favorable treatment; group 2, rats received transplantation of the fibrin matrix; group 3, animals were transplanted with NSCs embedded in the fibrin matrix; group 4, transplanted with the fibrin matrix containing 50 μg/mL CQDs; group 5, implanted with the fibrin matrix containing NSCs and 50 μg/mL CQDs; group 6, received transplantation of the fibrin matrix containing NSCs pretreated with 50 μg/mL CQDs for 4 days. Then the lesion was closed layer by layer. After surgery, the rats received an antibiotic injection for five consecutive days and manual bladder expression twice daily. A 30 μL of 10 mg/mL fibrinogen was mixed with 10 μL of thrombin (50 U/mL) and then solidified at 37 °C for 30 min to prepare the fibrin mentioned above matrix. The NSCs were mixed with the fibrinogen solution in advance, and each fibrin block eventually contained about 100 neurospheres. For the sixth group, NSCs were treated with 50 μg/mL CQDs in vitro for 4 days, and the pretreated NSCs were embedded in the fibrin matrix and transplanted into the spinal cord transection injury site.

Terminal-deoxynucleoitidyl Transferase Mediated Nick End Labeling (TUNEL) analysis was performed to determine the apoptosis of NSCs three days after transplantation using an Apoptosis Detection Kit (FITC) according to the manufacturer's instructions.

The animals were put in a TSE Multi Conditioning System (Germany) two months after transplantation to evaluate the locomotor improvement. This system is a modular integrated multipurpose solution supporting various behavioral paradigms on one single hardware and software platform, which can make an objective and overall evaluation of the spontaneous motor activity of the animals. Briefly, the rats could walk freely in the built-in clean container with a smooth floor (100 cm ×100 cm square, 60 cm wall height) for 5 min. During this period, the system automatically recorded all the motor activity parameters and simulated the trajectory of the rats' movement.

Two months after surgery, rats in each group were anesthetized with an intraperitoneal injection of pentobarbital sodium (0.05 g/kg) and perfused via the left ventricle with PBS followed by freshly prepared 4 % paraformaldehyde. The entire intact spinal cord was removed from the vertebra and kept in 4 % paraformaldehyde. After 3 days of fixation at 4 °C, a 3-cm-long horizontal section containing the graft site was cut from each spinal cord, and continuous slices with a thickness of 3 μm were cut longitudinally by a paraffin slicer (Leica RM2235, Wetzlar, Germany) and mounted on PLL-coated glass slides (Citotest, Jiangsu, China). Immunofluorescence double-staining against GFAP and NF-H was performed to evaluate the formation of neural relays as well as astroglial scars in the lesion. The sections were observed under an immunofluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan).

All experiments were conducted with three technical replicates (e.g., three 24- and 6-wells) from the same treatment. Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism7 (La Jolla, California). The data were statistically evaluated using unpaired t-test, one-way ANOVA variance followed by Tukey's post-hoc test, or Two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons tests. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The raw sequence data reported in this paper have been deposited in the Genome Sequence Archive (Genomics, Proteomics & Bioinformatics 2021) in National Genomics Data Center (Nucleic Acids Res 2022), China National Center for Bioinformation/Beijing Institute of Genomics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (GSA: CRA019807) that are publicly accessible at https://ngdc.cncb.ac.cn/gsa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: L. barbarum oligosaccharide (LBOS) is isolated and characterized
ce:section.ce:para: A crude extract of L. barbarum saccharides was prepared using water extraction and alcohol precipitation, followed by deproteinization. This was further purified with the AB-8 macroporous adsorption resin and DEAE-52 cellulose resin, followed by column chromatography, resulting in a single symmetric peak in the high-performance gel permeation chromatography (HPGPC) spectrum (Fig. 1a). The saccharide and free protein contents of the purified L. barbarum saccharide extract were 65.80 % ± 1.46 % and 0.14 % ± 0.17 %, respectively. The results of the sulfuric acid-carbazole analysis showed that the refined product contained 30.50 % ± 0.67 % of uronic acid.

According to the mass spectrum, the quasi-molecular ion peak at m/z 862.003 (<1800 Da) indicated that the collected eluates were oligosaccharides composed of fewer than 10 monomers (Fig. 1b). This L. barbarum oligosaccharide is abbreviated as LBOS. The results of 1-phenyl-3-methyl-5-pyrazolone (PMP) pre-column derivatization high-performance chromatography (HPLC) showed that LBOS was composed of D-galactose and D-galacturonic acid (Fig. 1c). The periodate oxidation revealed that the galactose residues were 1→6, (54.16 %)/1→3 (40.09 %)/1→2,4 (5.75 %) linked (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared from LBOS via one-step hydrothermal carbonization
ce:section.ce:para: Following one-step hydrothermal carbonization of the mixture of LBOS, PEI, and ED, the residual free ED was removed by rotary evaporation, and the remaining unreacted PEI (MW = 1200 Da) was separated by dialysis (molecular weight cut-off: 300 Da). The external dialysate was collected and examined by 1H NMR spectroscopy using the original LBOS, PEI, ED, and crude CQDs, as controls. As shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary information, the CQDs had chemical shifts at δ2.6–3.8 ppm, and LBOS, PEI, and ED at δ3.1–4.0 ppm, δ2.4–2.6 ppm, and δ2.5–2.6 ppm, respectively. The CQDs gathered shifts in LBOS, PEI, and ED. It is noteworthy that the chemical shift of LBOS in the CQDs (δ3.0–3.7 ppm) was upfield compared to that of the original LBOS (δ3.1–4.0 ppm), whereas the shifts of PEI and ED in the CQDs (δ2.6–3.0 ppm) were slightly downfield compared to those of the original PEI (δ2.4–2.6 ppm) and ED (δ2.5–2.6 ppm). This is likely due to the protonation of LBOS and deprotonation of PEI and ED during the hydrothermal carbonization process, which is consistent with a previous report [38]. Moreover, when compared with the crude CQDs, there was almost no peak of free PEI or ED at δ2.4–2.6 ppm in the dialyzed CQDs, indicating complete removal of unreacted PEI and ED in the dialyzed CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs are characterized by a series of analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, CQDs dissolved in water emitted bright blue fluorescence when illuminated using an ultraviolet lamp at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 2a). The photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield of CDs was 20.77 % with quinine sulfate as the reference. The UV–vis spectrum of the CQDs showed an absorption peak at 290 nm (Fig. 2b). It is well documented that the n–π* transition of the H-C=O groups on galactose could generate an absorption peak at 275–295 nm. In the present study, LBOS contained abundant galactose units, and the CQDs derived from LBOS likely retained some of these galactose units, thus giving rise to a peak at 290 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Meanwhile, the PL excitation and emission spectra showed maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 390 and 470 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b). The excitation-dependent PL emission spectra demonstrated that when the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 440 nm, the emission peaks of the CQDs shifted from 465 to 479 nm, with the maximum emission peak appearing at 470 nm when excited at 390 nm (Fig. 2c). This observation was consistent with the PL excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 2b). As previously documented [39], excitation-dependent PL emission generally involves the optical selection of size-dependent quantum effects and emissive traps on the CQD surface.

The organic functional groups on the surface of the CQDs were detected using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). Compared with the spectrum of LBOS, the broad peak at 3410 cm−1 in the spectrum of CQDs could be attributed to the overlapping effect of -NH from PEI and ED and the -OH from LBOS. In addition to the similar peaks to those of LBOS at 2930 (-CH stretching vibration) and 1640 cm−1 (-C=O stretching vibration), the spectrum of the CQDs showed new peaks at 1560 and 1480 cm−1 (CN stretching vibration). This result indicates that the CQDs maintained some structural properties of the original LBOS, and the cationic reagents PEI and ED were chemically bonded to the CQDs during hydrothermal carbonization.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy was employed to observe the morphology and size of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2e, the uniformly dispersed CQDs had a spherical shape and ultrasmall diameter (∼1 nm). The zeta potential of the CQDs was measured using agarose gel electrophoresis. As shown in Fig. 2f, the negatively charged plasmid-enhanced green fluorescent protein (pEGFP) was retained in the pore by the CQDs, and there was a trend of migration to the positive pole with increasing concentration of the CQDs (Fig. 2f, well 12–16). This result indicates that the CQDs had a positive surface charge, which would stimulate neurite outgrowth [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of NSCs is induced by CQDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The neurospheres isolated from the brains of neonatal rats were spherical and intensively expressed NSC markers including nestin and Sox2 (Fig. 3a). We conducted 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay (see Experimental Section) to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the CQDs at various concentrations (ranging from 0 to 300 μg/mL), after 4 days of incubation. The results showed that the CQDs had no apparent cytotoxic effect on NSCs at concentrations lower than 60 μg/mL (Fig. 3b). Based on this, we used 50 μg/mL CQDs in subsequent experiments (unless otherwise stated). At the same time, we found that the NSCs incubated with CQDs (50 μg/mL) displayed bright blue fluorescence, but were fragile green and showed almost no red fluorescence when observed using a laser confocal microscope, at excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 514 nm, respectively (Fig. 3c). We observed that CQDs (50 μg/mL) greatly facilitated the out-migration of differentiated cells from the neurospheres when compared with that of the blank control and LBOS (50 μg/mL) groups (Fig. S2a-b). Moreover, there were more typical neuron-like cells, with longer neurite extensions, from neurospheres in the CQD-treated group (Fig. 3d).

We conducted an immunofluorescence staining assay to detect NSC differentiation into neurons or astrocytes, with or without CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4a-b, the respective proportions of Tuj1+/GFAP- (class III beta-tubulin/GFAP-) and MAP2+/ GFAP- (microtubule-associated protein 2/GFAP-) neurons, among the total cells migrating out of the NSC spheres, were gradually enhanced. Simultaneously, GFAP + astrocytes were significantly diminished in a CQDs dose-dependent manner. Additionally, there was a similar increase in the expression of neuronal-specific markers, such as DCX (doublecortin) and NF-H (neurofilament heavy chain), but a significant decrease in GFAP expression after 4 days of treatment with CQDs (Fig. S3). Moreover, western blot results showed that, compared to the control group, there was an increased expression of neuronal markers, including Tuj1 and MAP2, in NSCs treated with CQDs and LBOS. However, the CQD-treated group showed a significant decrease in GFAP expression, while the LBOS-treated NSCs showed no noticeable change in GFAP expression (Fig. 4c). This observation implied that LBOS could enhance the neuronal differentiation of NSCs to some extent, but had little effect on inhibiting GFAP expression. In contrast, the LBOS-originated CQDs not only retained LBOS' role of improving NSCs' neuronal differentiation but also had the additional effect of inhibiting GFAP expression.

To delve deeper into the influence of CQDs on NSC differentiation, we performed RNA transcriptome sequencing (Fig. 5a and b). The differential gene analysis revealed a distinct pattern of gene expression changes following CQD treatment (Fig. 5c). A significant upregulation of genes associated with neuronal differentiation and maturation, such as Tubb3, Gap43, Syn1, Snap25, Nefh, Gad1, and Grin1, suggests that CQDs promote the transition of NSCs towards a mature neuronal phenotype. This is further supported by the upregulation of Neurog2, a key regulator of neuronal fate determination. Concurrently, the downregulation of genes associated with extracellular matrix components and mesenchymal lineages, including Col1a2, Col4a11, Col4a2, Col5a1, Snai2, Tgfb2, and Tnn, indicates that CQDs may suppress fibrosis-related processes, potentially reducing scar formation and promoting a more conducive environment for neuronal regeneration. The observed downregulation of TGF pathway-related genes is particularly noteworthy as it aligns with the known role of TGF-beta signaling in promoting fibrosis and inhibiting neuronal differentiation. These findings collectively suggest that CQDs promote a neurogenic microenvironment by stimulating neuronal differentiation while simultaneously inhibiting fibrotic processes, which could be crucial for enhancing functional recovery after spinal cord injury.

Intriguingly, although the gene expression of GFAP, an astroglial marker, remained unchanged following CQD treatment, immunofluorescence and Western blot results indicated a reduction in GFAP protein levels. This discrepancy suggests that CQDs may not directly influence GFAP transcription but instead interfere with or inhibit the protein translation process of GFAP. This finding warrants further investigation to elucidate the specific mechanisms by which CQDs selectively impact GFAP protein expression, potentially uncovering novel pathways for controlling glial differentiation and promoting neuronal regeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GFAP expression in astrocytes was specifically inhibited by CQDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: GFAP is an intermediate filament protein present in astrocytes in the central nervous system, enteric glial cells, and non-myelinating Schwann cells in the peripheral nervous system [41]. According to differences in size and amino acid sequences, intermediate filaments are classified into 6 subtypes (types I-VI), and GFAP is a member of III intermediate filaments [42]. Astrocytes, the most abundant cells in the vertebrate spinal cord, have numerous functions, including providing structural and nutritional support for neurons, maintaining homeostasis, immune surveillance, and detoxification [43]. In response to acute damage in the spinal cord, astrocytes become a reactive phenotype with excessive proliferation and exert neurotoxic effects [44,45]. Activated astrocytes overexpress GFAP, resulting in entangled cells that form dense glial scars and inhibit axonal re-growth. GFAP overexpression is believed to be critically important for the formation of thickened and extended processes of the activated astrocytes [41]. To date, a variety of approaches have been reported to alleviate the harmful effects of the GFAP -overexpressing astrocytes [46,47].

To understand whether PEI and ED played a role in inhibiting GFAP expression, CQDs were prepared with only PEI or ED, as well as LBOS+PEI or LBOS+ED. The results of immunofluorescence staining showed that in comparison with the blank control, the CQDs prepared with only PEI (PEI-CQDs) had a greater effect on inhibiting GFAP expression than those with ED alone (ED-CQDs), but neither displayed any improvement in neuronal differentiation (Fig. 6a-c). Consistent with the western blot results, the free LBOS had a certain effect on promoting NSC differentiation into MAP2+ neurons (Fig. 6d) compared with the control (Fig. 6a). When compared with the LBOS, the CQDs prepared with LBOS and PEI (LBOS+PEI-CQDs) exhibited reduced GFAP+ astrocytes and compromised MAP2+ neurons as well, while those derived from LBOS and ED (LBOS+ED-CQDs) showed a certain improvement in promoting neuronal differentiation but little effect on inhibiting astroglial (Fig. 6e and f). Hence, both ED and PEI are indispensable for CQDs as the ED might be able to preserve the neuronal promotion effect of LBOS while PEI modification might help inhibit GFAP expression.

Furthermore, we examined the expression of other cytoskeleton proteins including β-tubulin (a microtubule protein), vimentin (another type III intermediate filament expressed in astrocytes), and F-actin, in astrocytes after treatment with CQDs, to determine whether the CQDs have a specific effect on suppressing the expression of GFAP. As shown in Fig. 6g, after 8 days of incubation in the medium containing CQDs at various concentrations, the expression of GFAP decreased significantly with the increase in CQD concentration. However, as a type III intermediate filament, the expression of vimentin was hardly affected by CQDs, and the expression of tubulin and F-actin was not affected either. This observation indicated that the CQDs had a specific effect on inhibiting GFAP expression. The downregulation of GFAP in our study suggests that the neural stem cells are preferentially differentiating towards neuronal lineages rather than astrocytic lineages. This finding is significant as it implies that our experimental conditions or interventions promote neurogenesis and may hold potential for therapeutic applications in conditions where neuronal regeneration is desired. Further investigation into the specific factors or mechanisms responsible for the downregulation of GFAP in our model system is warranted to better understand the underlying molecular processes driving neuronal differentiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs promote axonal regeneration and function recovery after SCI in rats
ce:section.ce:para: To examine whether the CQDs can promote nerve regeneration and functional recovery after SCI, the CQDs and/or NSCs were embedded in a fibrin matrix and then transplanted into the complete transection lesion of the rat spinal cord. Two months later, animals underwent functional studies using a Multi Conditioning System (TSE Systems, Berlin, Germany) and were then anesthetized, and perfused with 4 % paraformaldehyde, before removal of the intact spinal cord.

We employed the TSE Multi Conditioning System to evaluate the locomotor activity of the animals for functional recovery assessment. The results revealed that the animals implanted with fibrin containing NSCs and CQDs (SCI+F+N+C group) or fibrin with CQD-pretreated NSCs (SCI+F+CN group) showed remarkably more complicated movement trajectories than the animals in other groups (Fig. 7a). In addition, these two groups had significantly increased moving time, total distance, and center distance, when compared with the SCI group (Fig. 7b-d). Furthermore, the time clockwise (CW)/counter-clockwise (CCW) ratio in these two groups (NSCs plus CQDs or NSCs treated with CQDs) was closer to 1.0 compared to that of the other groups (Fig. 7e). There was no obvious difference in the motor function of the rats between the two groups. The “time of movement” was the time of animal movement for a total duration of 5 min. The “total distance” refers to the total movement distance that the animals made in the entire detection area during the observation period. The “center distance” is the traveling distance that animals travel in the central area of the field (center size: 50 %). The “time CW/CCW” represents the ratio of the time of clockwise to counterclockwise rotation. These parameters reflect the spontaneous movement ability of the tested animals. Longer time moving, total distance, and center distance suggest better motor function. The closer the time CW/CCW is to 1.0, the closer the locomotor activity becomes to that of normal animals. These results indicate that CQDs, together with exogenous NSCs, can significantly improve motor function recovery after SCI (additional movie files show this in more detail in the Supplementary information). This enhanced movement complexity, characterized by increased variability and dynamic range, indicates a greater degree of motor control and coordination, likely stemming from the combined therapeutic effects of the NSCs and CQDs. The presence of NSCs within the fibrin matrix may promote neurogenesis and axonal regeneration, while the CQDs could enhance neuronal differentiation. This combined effect, potentially amplified by the CQD pre-treatment of NSCs, could be responsible for the observed increase in movement complexity and suggests a synergistic benefit in utilizing both NSCs and CQDs for spinal cord injury treatment. Further investigation is warranted to elucidate the specific mechanisms underlying this enhanced functional recovery and assess its long-term implications for improving neurological function in SCI patients.

To detect the survival of NSCs in the fibrin matrix, the results of Tunel staining showed that only a few apoptotic cells were found in the NSC scaffolds 3 days after transplantation into the spinal cord injury site, and most of the cells had good vitality (Fig. S4). To examine the nerve regeneration in vivo, the damaged spinal cord segments were paraffin-embedded and immunolabeled with GFAP and neuronal markers such as NF-H, NeuN, and TUJ1 for anatomical analysis. In the SCI animals, partial spinal cord tissue was squeezed into the lesion from the two cut ends of the spinal cord, and a cavity surrounded by a dense GFAP + astroglial scar had been formed at the center of the lesion (Fig. 8a, arrows). This glial scar is a physical barrier that prevents axonal regrowth and is a source of chemical inhibitors for nerve regeneration. In animals treated with fibrin matrix alone (SCI+F group), the inward collapse of spinal tissue from both ends was greatly alleviated, and a certain number of GFAP + astrocytes migrated into the gap, but there was no apparent axonal extension across the lesion (Fig. 8b). This result indicated that the fibrin matrix could provide specific mechanical support for nerve repair, but additional stimuli were necessary for axonal regrowth. When CQDs were added to the fibrin matrix (SCI+F+C group), numerous NF-H+ neurofilaments, instead of GFAP + astrocytes, extended from both ends (Fig. 8c) into the gap, despite no apparent axonal reconnection. This observation suggests that CQDs might enhance the neuronal differentiation of host NSCs and facilitate axonal elongation. However, the addition of CQDs alone to the fibrin matrix was not sufficient for axonal reconnection at the time of investigation.

To further improve the functional recovery, exogenous NSCs were added to the implants. The NSCs alone embedded in the fibrin matrix (SCI+F+N group) failed to differentiate into neurons that could bridge the gap, and the two cut ends were mostly GFAP+ astrocytes (Fig. 8d, shown by asterisks). When NSCs were mixed with CQDs (SCI+F+N+C group) (Fig. 8e) or pretreated with CQDs for 4 days (SCI+F+CN group) in the implants (Fig. 8f), the grafted cells primarily differentiated into neurons that extensively expressed the axonal marker neurofilament NF-H. These axons extended across the lesion gap into the host spinal tissue in both the rostral and caudal directions (Fig. 8e-f, arrowheads), corresponding to the greatly improved motor function of these two groups. Moreover, there was a significant increase in the expression of NF-H in the host spinal tissue, both rostral and caudal to the lesion, in both groups (Fig. 8e-f, indicated by asterisks). This finding implies that either mixing NSCs with CQDs in a fibrin matrix or pre-treating NSCs with CQDs before implantation may enhance their neuronal differentiation potential and promote a more sustained neurodegenerative response. This observation points towards possible approaches for future therapeutic strategies, potentially leading to enhanced long-term functional recovery in spinal cord injury patients. Further investigation into the mechanisms underlying this enhanced stability and the optimal timing and duration of CQD pre-treatment is warranted to optimize the therapeutic potential of this promising combination.

This finding aligns with the greatly improved motor function observed in these two groups, suggesting a direct correlation between enhanced axonal regeneration and functional recovery. This research demonstrates the promise of using CQDs in conjunction with NSCs for therapeutic interventions in spinal cord injury, opening new avenues for promoting functional recovery and restoring neurological function.

In this study, for the first time, we prepared a type of CQDs via a “bottom-up” method using oligosaccharides from L. barbarum (LBOS) as the carbon source, with the PEI and ED being the cationic reagents. The resultant LBOS-derived CQDs possessed an ultrasmall size and a positive surface charge. These CQDs showed a specific inhibitory effect on the expression of glial scar marker GFAP while enhancing the neuronal specialization of NSCs in vitro. Furthermore, after transplantation with the fibrin matrix containing the CQDs and exogenous NSCs into complete transection lesions of the spinal cord, the rats exhibited significantly improved motor function recovery. Immunofluorescence histochemical staining revealed that CQDs enhanced neuronal activity while inhibiting the GFAP expression of exogenous NSCs. Collectively, this work presents the first evidence of employing CQDs in regulating NSC differentiation, thus proving a paradigm for exploring the bioactivity of quantum dots.
